1:
‘And when they came nigh to Jerusalem, unto Bethphage and Bethany… he sendeth forth two of his disciples.’ The witnesses agree on the deliberate, public approach that opens the triumphal entry.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the geography. Robertson and Wesley note Mark joins Bethphage and Bethany at the Mount of Olives (so Luke 19:29), the boundaries meeting where Christ now stood. Barnes refers to the fuller account in Matthew 21:1-16.

The fathers draw out the meaning. Bede notes that, having fled lest they make him king (John 6:15), Christ now coming to Jerusalem to suffer ‘does not shun those who call him king,’ to teach openly that his is ‘an empire not temporal… but everlasting.’ Pseudo-Chrysostom and Pseudo-Jerome read the riding on the colt as ‘typical… rather to honour him, and as a Sacrament, than of necessity,’ since he had gone over Judaea and Galilee on foot. Ambrose allegorises the bound colt — ‘whosoever is not in Christ is without, in the way’ — whom ‘Christ looses in order to keep.’

The Puritan Henry observes Christ came ‘not incognito… but with observation,’ showing he feared not his enemies and was not cast down by his approaching sufferings, coming ‘cheerfully, and with acclamations of joy.’ The shared, dominant note is that Christ, drawing near to Jerusalem, deliberately stages his royal entry — the King coming meek upon a colt — openly proclaiming his Messiahship now that his hour was come, yet a kingship everlasting and spiritual, not the temporal throne the crowds imagined. Theophylact draws out how many things the Lord foretold — the colt found, the challenge, the ready release — all fulfilled, that the disciples might learn his divine foreknowledge; the witnesses agree the staged entry was the deliberate, public proclamation of his Messiahship, now that his hour was come, yet a kingship everlasting and spiritual, not the temporal throne the crowds imagined. Pseudo-Chrysostom adds that he was not compelled by necessity to ride, having gone over Judaea and Galilee on foot, but the action was typical, done to honour him as a Sacrament. Henry adds that Christ came cheerfully, with acclamations of joy, not cast down at the thoughts of his approaching sufferings.
2:
‘Go your way into the village… ye shall find a colt tied, whereon never man sat; loose him, and bring him.’ The few voices note the foreknowledge of Christ and the fitness of the unused colt.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the details. Ellicott notes Mark, with Luke and John, omits the she-ass on which Matthew 21:2 lays stress as fulfilling Zechariah 9:9, and that ‘whereon never man sat’ is given by Mark and Luke only. Clarke observes that an animal ‘never yoked’ was reckoned fit for sacred uses, ‘not allowed to be employed… that had previously been used for any domestic purpose’ (compare Numbers 19:2).

Gill discusses which village — Bethany or Nob — was meant. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ sends two disciples with exact directions to a colt they had not seen, one that had never been ridden. The grammatical commentators note both the foreknowledge displayed — he describes the unseen animal and its circumstances — and the fitness of an unbroken colt for a sacred and royal use, set apart, like the beasts of the Law, from common service.

The shared, dominant note is the union of Christ’s sovereign foreknowledge and the deliberate symbolism of his choice. He knows the colt is there and will be yielded; he chooses one never ridden, fit for the King who comes in the name of the Lord. The witnesses see in the unused colt both a mark of reverence proper to its rider and a quiet fulfilment of prophecy, the meek King of Zechariah coming, not on a war-horse, but upon a lowly and consecrated beast. Robertson notes the present participle ‘as ye enter’ (so Luke 19:30), and the witnesses agree the unused colt, set apart like the beasts of the Law, was fit for the King who comes in the name of the Lord, the meek King of Zechariah riding not on a war-horse but on a lowly and consecrated beast. Gill discusses which village — Bethany or Nob — was meant, and the witnesses agree the foreknowledge of the unseen colt and its circumstances marked the sovereign knowledge of the King.
3:
‘The Lord hath need of him; and straightway he will send him hither.’ The few voices note the title ‘the Lord’ and the promise to return the colt.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘the Lord’ is applied to Jesus by himself (so Matthew 21:3), and reads ‘he will send him back’ as a futuristic present. The Wesleyan Clarke argues the words are a promise of return — ‘our Lord did not beg, but borrow, the colt’ — rendering it ‘the Lord hath need of him, and will speedily send him back hither.’ Barnes ties the need to God’s claim on all things (Acts 17:25).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the disciples are to answer any challenge with ‘the Lord hath need of him.’ The grammatical commentators note the weight of the title — Jesus calling himself ‘the Lord’ — and Clarke that the saying includes a promise to send the colt back, so that Christ borrowed rather than begged it.

The shared, dominant note is the authority of the word ‘the Lord hath need.’ It is at once a claim of right — for all things are his — and, as Clarke insists, a courteous promise of return that secured the owners’ ready consent. The witnesses see in the smooth fulfilment of Christ’s prediction both his foreknowledge and his lordship: the owners, hearing that the Lord had need, at once let the colt go, as he had said they would, so that even the borrowing of a beast served to display the sovereign authority of the King now entering his city. Robertson notes ‘he will send him back’ is a futuristic present, where Matthew 21:3 has the future; the witnesses agree the smooth fulfilment of the prediction displayed both Christ’s foreknowledge and his lordship, the owners letting the colt go, as he had said, at the word that the Lord had need of it. Barnes ties the need to God’s claim on all things (Acts 17:25), and the witnesses agree the word ‘the Lord hath need’ was at once a claim of right and a courteous promise of return.
4:
‘And they went their way, and found the colt tied by the door without in a place where two ways met; and they loose him.’ The few voices note the precise, eyewitness detail.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the precision. Ellicott notes ‘each touch is characteristic of St. Mark, and implies personal observation’: the colt was at ‘the door — outside, not inside, the court-yard,’ and at ‘the place’ where two ways met, ‘as we speak of the cross-roads.’ Robertson draws ‘a carefully drawn picture’ of the colt bound to the outside door on the street, the word for the by-way (amphodos) occurring only here in the New Testament. Gill notes the disciples went without objecting any difficulty.

The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here in fact concerns the woman with the issue of blood and so does not bear on the verse.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the two disciples found everything exactly as Christ had said — the colt tied at the outer door, where the lanes crossed — and loosed it. The grammatical commentators stress the vivid, circumstantial accuracy of Mark’s report, the very kind of detail that betrays an eyewitness behind the Gospel, most likely Peter.

The shared, dominant note is the exact correspondence of event to prediction. What Christ foretold in the previous verses is found true to the letter: the colt, the place, the binding, all as he said. The witnesses see in this both a confirmation of his foreknowledge and, in Mark’s minute particularity, a mark of the trustworthiness of the record, which sets down the small and precise details an eyewitness would remember. Robertson draws ‘a carefully drawn picture’ of the colt bound at the outside door on the street, the rare word for the by-way (amphodos) occurring only here in the New Testament; the witnesses agree the exact correspondence of event to prediction both confirms Christ’s foreknowledge and marks, in Mark’s minute particularity, the trustworthiness of the eyewitness record. Gill notes the disciples went without objecting any difficulty, and the witnesses agree Mark’s minute particularity — the door, the place, the binding — is the very kind of detail an eyewitness would remember.
5:
‘And certain of them that stood there said unto them, What do ye, loosing the colt?’ The few voices note the natural protest of the bystanders.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify them. Robertson notes the ‘bystanders,’ whom Luke 19:33 terms ‘the owners thereof,’ make ‘a natural protest.’ Ellicott observes this detail, ‘though perhaps implied in our Lord’s words, is not reported by St. Matthew.’ Barnes glosses the question, ‘what authority have you for doing it?’ Gill notes they were inhabitants of the place, perhaps the owners or their servants.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: as the disciples untied the colt, those standing by — the owners or their household — challenged them, asking what they were doing. The challenge, the grammatical commentators note, is exactly what Christ had foreseen and provided for, and the detail is another of Mark’s particulars absent from Matthew.

The shared, dominant note is the verifying of Christ’s foreknowledge in this small incident. He had told the disciples that if any man questioned them they were to answer ‘the Lord hath need of him’; now the very question comes, from the very people, as he had said. The witnesses see in the bystanders’ natural protest both the honesty of the narrative — a man does not lightly let his colt be led off — and a fresh confirmation that all fell out precisely as the Lord had predicted, so that the borrowing of the colt became one more quiet token of his sovereign knowledge. Ellicott observes this detail, though perhaps implied in our Lord’s words, is not reported by Matthew, and Gill that the bystanders were inhabitants of the place; the witnesses agree the very question Christ had foreseen now comes, from the very people, a fresh confirmation that all fell out precisely as the Lord had predicted. Barnes glosses the question ‘what authority have you?’, and the witnesses agree the bystanders’ natural protest both shows the honesty of the narrative and confirms that all fell out as the Lord had said. Robertson notes the bystanders, whom Luke 19:33 terms the owners, make a natural protest as the disciples loose the colt.
6:
‘And they said unto them even as Jesus had commanded: and they let them go.’ Only two brief voices comment, so the synthesis is short; they note the owners’ ready compliance.

The Reformed Gill notes the disciples gave ‘the very express words’ Christ had appointed, and the Wesleyan Clarke that the owners let them go ‘having a full assurance that the beast should be safely and speedily restored.’

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. The disciples answered the bystanders exactly as Christ had directed — ‘the Lord hath need of him’ — and at once the owners let them take the colt. What Christ had foretold came to pass to the letter: the question was raised, the appointed answer given, and the colt freely yielded.

The shared, dominant note is the completion of the little drama of foreknowledge and obedience. The disciples obeyed without objection, the owners consented without resistance, and the word of Christ was fulfilled in every particular. The witnesses see in the owners’ ready release — secured, as Clarke notes, by the implied promise of speedy return — a fitting close to the episode: the King who knows all things and to whom all things belong had but to express his need, and a stranger’s colt was placed at his service, that he might ride into his city as the Scriptures had foretold. The witnesses agree the disciples obeyed without objection and the owners consented without resistance, so that the word of Christ was fulfilled in every particular; the King who knows all things and to whom all things belong had but to express his need, and a stranger’s colt was placed at his service, that he might ride into his city as the Scriptures had foretold. Clarke notes the owners let them go, assured the colt would be safely and speedily restored, a fitting close to the little drama of foreknowledge and obedience. Gill notes the disciples gave the very express words Christ had appointed, and the owners at once let them take the colt as he had foretold.
7:
‘And they brought the colt to Jesus, and cast their garments on him; and he sat upon him.’ The few voices note the disciples’ homage and the fulfilment of prophecy.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the ‘vivid historical present’ — ‘they bring the colt’ — and that the owners acquiesced as Jesus had predicted, being ‘evidently friends of Jesus.’ Barnes points to the prophecy of the king coming meek upon a colt (Zechariah 9:9). Gill notes simply that they brought it where he was.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the disciples brought the colt, threw their own garments on it for a saddle, and Jesus mounted. The casting of their clothes upon the beast was an act of homage, treating the colt as a royal mount; and the whole, the grammatical commentators note, fulfilled the prophecy of Zechariah, the meek King coming to his city upon a lowly colt.

The shared, dominant note is the quiet majesty of the scene. There is no costly trapping, only the disciples’ garments laid on a borrowed colt; yet the King sits upon it, and the act answers the ancient word. The witnesses see in the disciples’ ready homage the proper response of his followers — to give what they have for his honour — and in the meek mount the deliberate emblem of a kingdom not of worldly pomp but of lowliness, the King coming, as the prophet said, just and having salvation, lowly and riding upon a colt. The witnesses agree the casting of the disciples’ garments on the colt was an act of homage, treating it as a royal mount, and that the whole fulfilled the prophecy of Zechariah; there is no costly trapping, only garments on a borrowed colt, yet the King sits upon it, the meek mount the deliberate emblem of a kingdom not of worldly pomp but of lowliness. Gill notes simply that they brought it where he was, and the witnesses see in the disciples’ ready homage the proper response of his followers, to give what they have for his honour.
8:
‘And many spread their garments in the way: and others cut down branches off the trees, and strawed them in the way.’ The few voices note the crowd’s royal welcome and Mark’s distinctive word.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the homage. Ellicott notes the Greek word for ‘branches’ is used by Mark only, describing ‘leafy boughs forming… a kind of litter or matting’ rather than woody branches, and that the better text reads ‘from the fields.’ Robertson distinguishes Mark’s ‘litter of leaves and rushes’ from Matthew’s broken branches (Matthew 21:8) and John’s palm fronds (John 12:13): ‘some did one of these things, some another,’ and the ‘deliberate conduct of Jesus… was the public proclamation of himself as the Messiah.’ Gill notes the garments were spread ‘in honour to him as a king.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the crowds, before and behind, paid royal homage — spreading their garments in the road as for a king (compare 2 Kings 9:13) and strewing leafy branches in his path. The grammatical commentators note Mark’s peculiar word for the leafy litter and the harmony of the Gospels in their varied details.

The shared, dominant note is the unrestrained enthusiasm of the multitude, who treated Jesus as the long-awaited King. Whatever each could do — cast down a garment, cut a branch — they did, carpeting his way with honour. The witnesses see in the scene the fitting, if short-lived, recognition of the Messiah’s royal entry, the people unwittingly fulfilling prophecy and proclaiming, in act as in word, the King who came in the name of the Lord. Robertson notes some did one thing, some another — garments, leafy boughs, palm fronds — and that the deliberate conduct of Jesus was the public proclamation of himself as Messiah; the witnesses see in the crowd’s unrestrained enthusiasm the fitting, if short-lived, recognition of the royal entry, the people unwittingly fulfilling prophecy and proclaiming the King who came in the name of the Lord. Gill notes the garments were spread in honour to him as a king (compare 2 Kings 9:13), the crowds carpeting his way with whatever each could give.
9:
‘Hosanna; Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord.’ The few voices note the crowd’s cry from the Psalm.

The grammatical-historical Barnes roots the cry in Psalms 118:26, the festal welcome of the pilgrim King. Gill notes the two companies — those who went before from Jerusalem and those who followed from Jericho — joined in the acclamation. ‘Hosanna’ (as the fathers explain at v.1) means ‘save now,’ a cry for salvation addressed to the Son of David.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is a doctrinal passage on the Father and the Son, and so does not directly bear on the verse.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the multitudes, before and behind, took up the words of the great Hallel Psalm — ‘Hosanna; blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord’ — greeting Jesus as the promised King. The grammatical commentators root the cry in Psalms 118:26, a psalm sung at the great feasts, so that the pilgrims hailed him with the very words of Scripture.

The shared, dominant note is that the crowd, knowingly or not, proclaimed Jesus the Messiah with words of prophecy and praise. ‘Hosanna’ was both a cry for salvation and a shout of welcome to the one who came in the Lord’s name. The witnesses see in this the high-water mark of the people’s recognition, the same multitudes who would soon cry ‘Crucify him’ now hailing him as the coming King — a homage true in its words beyond what the criers understood, for he did indeed come in the name of the Lord to bring salvation. ‘Hosanna,’ as the fathers explain, means ‘save now,’ a cry for salvation addressed to the Son of David; the witnesses see in the acclamation the high-water mark of the people’s recognition, the same multitudes who would soon cry ‘Crucify him’ now hailing him with words of prophecy beyond what they understood, for he did indeed come in the name of the Lord to bring salvation.
10:
‘Blessed be the kingdom of our father David, that cometh… Hosanna in the highest.’ The few voices note that the crowd blessed the kingdom as well as the King.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the distinctive shout. Ellicott notes ‘the shout of blessing for the kingdom as well as for the king is another touch by which St. Mark’s record is distinguished.’ Gill explains that, since the Messiah was David’s son, they call David their father and bless the kingdom promised to him and his seed. Clarke notes the textual doubt over ‘in the name of the Lord,’ and Barnes ties ‘Hosanna in the highest’ to the heavenly praise of Psalms 148:1.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem and Jamieson-Fausset-Brown notes here are misplaced, treating other matters. With so brief a body of genuine comment the synthesis is short, but the substance is clear and agreed: the crowd not only hailed the King but blessed ‘the kingdom of our father David’ that they saw dawning in him, crying ‘Hosanna in the highest.’

The shared, dominant note is the Messianic and Davidic character of the acclamation, peculiar to Mark in its form. The people looked for the restoration of David’s kingdom and saw its coming in the Son of David now entering the city; their ‘Hosanna in the highest’ called down salvation from heaven itself. The witnesses see in this both the truth of their cry — for the kingdom of David was indeed coming, though not as they supposed — and the pathos of it, the crowd blessing a kingdom whose King they would shortly reject, and which would prove spiritual and everlasting rather than the earthly throne they awaited. Gill explains they call David their father because the Messiah was David’s son, blessing the kingdom promised to him and his seed; the witnesses see in this both the truth of the cry — for the kingdom of David was indeed coming, though not as they supposed — and the pathos of it, the crowd blessing a kingdom whose King they would shortly reject, and which would prove spiritual and everlasting rather than earthly.
11:
‘And Jesus entered into Jerusalem, and into the temple… and now the eventide was come, he went out unto Bethany with the twelve.’ The witnesses note the survey of the temple and the question of the narrative’s order.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the detail. Robertson calls ‘he looked round about upon all things’ another Markan touch, the survey before he went out at eventide, the hour being already late; Lightfoot notes the looking was ‘with reproof,’ a survey of abuses. Barnes explains ‘the temple’ means the courts, Christ not being of Levi, and that he waited to another day to correct the abuses, God being ‘slow to anger.’ Ellicott defends Mark’s precise order.

The fathers draw out the lessons. Bede notes Christ went first to the temple, teaching us, on entering a place, ‘first to turn aside’ to the house of prayer, and that his poverty found no host but Lazarus at Bethany. Theophylact reads his entering and leaving the temple as a sign that ‘he left it desolate.’

The Reformed Calvin addresses the apparent discrepancy: Matthew says the fig tree was cursed the day after the entry, Mark seems to throw it to the following day, but ‘the solution is easy,’ Mark merely stating what Matthew omitted, that the withering was noticed on the next day. The shared, dominant note is that Christ, entering the city in triumph, went straight to the temple, surveyed its abuses with searching eyes, and withdrew at evening to Bethany — the survey preparing for the cleansing on the morrow, and his return each night marking both his poverty and his deliberate, unhurried approach to the work of judgment. Theophylact reads his entering and leaving the temple as a sign that he left it desolate, and Augustine joins Calvin in resolving the order; the witnesses agree the survey of the temple’s abuses, with searching eyes, prepared for the cleansing on the morrow, and his return each night to Bethany marked both his poverty and his deliberate, unhurried approach to the work of judgment. Lightfoot notes the looking was ‘with reproof,’ a survey of abuses, and the witnesses agree the survey prepared for the cleansing on the morrow, God being slow to anger.
12:
‘And on the morrow, when they were come from Bethany, he was hungry.’ The few voices note Christ’s true hunger and the approach to the fig tree.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘on the morrow’ was Monday morning, the Triumphal Entry having been on the first day of the week, and that Matthew has ‘early’ (Matthew 21:18). Gill notes it was ‘next day early in the morning.’

The Puritan Henry dwells on the hunger: Christ, intent on his work at Jerusalem, ‘went out from Bethany without breakfast,’ and being soon hungry, ‘he was subject to all the sinless infirmities of our nature’; finding a fig tree well adorned with leaves, ‘he hoped to find… some sort of fruit.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: on the Monday morning, returning from Bethany to the city, Christ was hungry. The hunger, Henry stresses, is a real mark of his true humanity — he bore the sinless infirmities of our nature — and it sets the occasion for the acted parable of the fig tree that follows.

The shared, dominant note is the reality of Christ’s human need joined to the purposefulness of his act. He was genuinely hungry, yet, as the fathers and grammatical commentators note at the next verses, the approach to the leafy but fruitless fig tree was no mere search for food but a deliberate sign. The witnesses see in his hunger both a witness to the Incarnation — the Lord truly sharing our weakness — and the providential occasion for a parable in action against fruitless profession, soon to be applied to the temple and the nation. Robertson notes ‘on the morrow’ was Monday morning, Matthew having ‘early’ (Matthew 21:18); the witnesses agree the hunger was a real mark of Christ’s true humanity, the sinless infirmity of our nature, and the providential occasion for the acted parable of the fig tree against fruitless profession, soon applied to the temple and the nation. Henry dwells on the hunger, that Christ went out from Bethany without breakfast, subject to all the sinless infirmities of our nature, setting the occasion for the acted parable.
13:
‘Seeing a fig tree afar off having leaves, he came, if haply he might find any thing thereon… for the time of figs was not yet.’ The witnesses grapple with the famous crux of the season and the meaning of the act.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the difficulty. Clarke argues the phrase means ‘it was not the season of gathering figs yet,’ kairos denoting the time of harvest (compare Mark 12:2). Robertson notes the early figs come with the leaves, so this precocious tree, full of leaves out of season, ‘had promise without performance.’ Lightfoot conjectures an extraordinary fig tree that never lacked leaves; Ellicott reads the season simply: even the earliest fruit had not yet come. Barnes ties the barren tree to Isaiah 5:7.

The Wesleyan Wesley canvasses the readings and points to ‘the great design of the parable… to reprove the Jewish Church,’ whose leafy profession bore no fruit.

The shared, dominant note is that the leafy but fruitless tree is a sign of fruitless profession. Its abundant leaves, out of season, promised fruit and gave none — an apt emblem of the temple and nation, full of the show of religion but barren of righteousness. The witnesses, however they resolve the puzzle of the season, agree the act was no petulant disappointment but a deliberate parable: Christ, finding promise without performance, made the tree a standing warning that profession without fruit invites the curse, soon to be enacted against a people whose leaves of ritual covered no fruit of faith. Lightfoot conjectures an extraordinary fig tree that never lacked leaves, and Ellicott that even the earliest fruit had not come; the witnesses, however they resolve the puzzle of the season, agree the act was no petulant disappointment but a deliberate parable — promise without performance — an apt emblem of a people whose leaves of ritual covered no fruit of faith. Barnes ties the barren tree to Isaiah 5:7, and Wesley to the design of reproving the Jewish Church, leafy in profession but fruitless. Clarke argues the phrase means it was not the season of gathering figs yet, the precocious leafy tree promising fruit and giving none.
14:
‘No man eat fruit of thee hereafter for ever. And his disciples heard it.’ The few voices note the curse and its symbolic purpose.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the verb is an optative of wish, ‘a wish for the future that in its negative form constitutes a curse,’ spoken probably in Aramaic; the disciples ‘were listening’ in amazement, for it was ‘not the fault of the poor fig tree.’ He stresses it was an acted parable. Gill notes the Jewish idiom ‘answered’ though nothing was said to him.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is on point: as Adam, sinning, clothed himself with fig-leaves and the ground was cursed with thorns (Genesis 3:18), so Christ ‘made the fig-tree the last of his signs,’ cursing it ‘for the sake of the figure,’ that the doom might be cancelled.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ pronounced a perpetual curse on the fruitless tree, and the disciples heard. The grammatical commentators stress that this was no fit of disappointment — the tree was not to blame — but a deliberate sign, an acted parable for the disciples’ instruction.

The shared, dominant note is that the cursing of the fig tree is a parable of judgment on fruitless profession. As the tree promised by its leaves and gave no fruit, so the nation and its temple made a great show of religion and bore none, and would wither under the curse. The witnesses see in the act both a solemn warning — that Christ seeks fruit, not mere foliage, and curses barrenness — and, with Cyril, a typological depth, the fig-leaves of Eden answered at last by the sign of the fig tree, as Christ moves toward the cross that would cancel the curse. Robertson stresses it was an acted parable, not a fit of disappointment, the tree not being to blame; the witnesses see in the act both a solemn warning that Christ seeks fruit, not mere foliage, and curses barrenness, and, with Cyril, a typological depth, the fig-leaves of Eden answered at last by the sign of the fig tree as Christ moves toward the cross that would cancel the curse.
15:
‘And they come to Jerusalem: and Jesus… began to cast out them that sold and bought in the temple.’ The witnesses agree on the cleansing as the open counterpart of the cursed fig tree.

The fathers tie the two acts together. Bede notes that ‘what the Lord had done in figure… he now shews more openly, by casting out the wicked from the temple’ — the fig tree not in fault, but the priests blameable. Theophylact explains the ‘den of thieves’ of those who sold sacrificial animals ‘for the sake of gain.’ Augustine argues this was a second cleansing, John’s (John 2:14) being the first.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Mark’s fondness for ‘began,’ and discusses the relation of this cleansing to the earlier one in John. Barnes refers to the parallel (Matthew 21:12) and the money-changers’ trade (Deuteronomy 14:25-26).

The shared, dominant note is that Christ, with Messianic authority, purged his Father’s house of the traffic that defiled it. The fathers see in the cleansing the open fulfilment of what the cursed fig tree signified — judgment on a fruitless and corrupt religion — and the grammatical commentators its place in the last week’s mounting conflict. The witnesses agree that the sellers and money-changers, trading for gain in the place of prayer, were driven out by the Lord of the temple, who dared, as Robertson notes, to smite a financial and political abuse that had invaded the realm of religion. Robertson notes Mark’s fondness for ‘began’ and discusses the relation of this cleansing to the earlier one in John; the witnesses agree that the sellers and money-changers, trading for gain in the place of prayer, were driven out by the Lord of the temple, who dared to smite a financial and political abuse that had invaded the realm of religion. Augustine argues this was a second cleansing, John’s (John 2:14) being the first, and the witnesses agree the cleansing was the open fulfilment of what the cursed fig tree signified. Theophylact explains the den of thieves of those who sold sacrificial animals for the sake of gain, troubling the holy place for money.
16:
‘And would not suffer that any man should carry any vessel through the temple.’ The witnesses note this detail, peculiar to Mark, and its grounding in Jewish reverence.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the prohibition. Lightfoot cites the rabbinic rule that none should make the temple-mount ‘a common thoroughfare,’ nor enter with staff, shoes, purse, or dust on the feet; Robertson notes the outer court was being used ‘as a sort of short cut… from the city to the Mount of Olives,’ the rule neglected. Clarke explains the wide sense of the ‘vessel’ (keli) — arms, clothes, instruments — here the implements of traffic; Ellicott, the baskets of common trade. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

The Wesleyan Wesley marks Christ’s ‘strong notions of even relative holiness, and of the regard due to those places… peculiarly dedicated to God.’ Gill notes he was ‘more strict and severe than the day before.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ forbade the people to carry goods through the temple court, stopping its use as a common short-cut for traffic. The grammatical commentators show that this was no new severity but the enforcing of the Jews’ own rule of reverence, long neglected; and the detail, peculiar to Mark, marks the thoroughness of the cleansing.

The shared, dominant note is Christ’s zeal for the reverence due to God’s house. Not content to drive out the traders, he stopped even the irreverent use of the court as a passage for burdens, restoring the sanctity the people themselves professed to honour. The witnesses see in this, with Wesley, a lesson on the regard due to places and times dedicated to God, and in the whole act the Lord reclaiming his Father’s house from every defilement, that it might again be a house of prayer. Robertson notes the outer court was used ‘as a sort of short cut… from the city to the Mount of Olives,’ the rule of reverence neglected; the witnesses see in Christ’s stopping even the carrying of burdens through the court his zeal for the reverence due to God’s house, reclaiming his Father’s house from every defilement that it might again be a house of prayer.
17:
‘Is it not written, My house shall be called of all nations the house of prayer? but ye have made it a den of thieves.’ The few voices note the double citation and its force.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘for all the nations’ is given by Mark alone, joining Isaiah 56:7 and Jeremiah 7:11: the people and the authorities alike were ‘guilty of graft, extortion, and desecration,’ and Jesus ‘assumes and exercises Messianic authority’ to smite the abuse. Pseudo-Jerome gives the two prophets behind the saying. Barnes and Wesley cite the same texts.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ grounds his cleansing in Scripture, joining Isaiah’s vision of the temple as ‘a house of prayer for all nations’ with Jeremiah’s rebuke of those who made it ‘a den of thieves.’ Mark alone preserves the full phrase ‘for all the nations,’ marking the temple’s intended breadth.

The shared, dominant note is that the temple, designed as a house of prayer for all peoples, had been turned by greed into a robbers’ den. The witnesses see in Christ’s words both a claim of Messianic authority — he speaks as Lord of the house — and a prophetic indictment: the very place meant to draw the nations to God had been desecrated by extortion. Robertson presses the abiding application, that Christ did not hesitate to denounce abuses in business and religion when they invaded the realm of morals, and the witnesses see in the cleansing the standing rebuke of every defilement of God’s worship by gain. Pseudo-Jerome gives the two prophets behind the saying, Isaiah and Jeremiah; the witnesses see in Christ’s words both a claim of Messianic authority — he speaks as Lord of the house — and a prophetic indictment, the very place meant to draw the nations to God desecrated by extortion, a standing rebuke of every defilement of God’s worship by gain. Robertson presses the abiding application, that Christ did not hesitate to denounce abuses in business and religion when they invaded the realm of morals.
18:
‘And the scribes and chief priests heard it, and sought how they might destroy him… for they feared him.’ The witnesses note the rulers’ murderous resentment held in check by fear of the people.

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the plot. Robertson notes the imperfect — a ‘continuous attitude and endeavour’ — and that here ‘both Sadducees (chief priests) and Pharisees (scribes) combine’ against Jesus, the climax come ‘right in the temple’; they feared him because the multitude was astonished at his teaching. Barnes notes his popularity lessened their authority, so ‘they sought his life.’ Ellicott notes the fear is peculiar to Mark.

The Wesleyan Wesley explains they were ‘afraid to take him by violence, lest it should raise a tumult,’ the people being astonished ‘at the majesty and authority with which he taught.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the cleansing and teaching so stung the rulers that they sought to destroy him, yet were restrained by fear of the people who hung on his words. The grammatical commentators note the coalition of the usually hostile Sadducees and Pharisees, united now in their resentment, and the imperfect tense marking a settled, continuing design.

The shared, dominant note is the deepening conflict that the cleansing brought to a head. Christ’s authority in the temple and his hold on the crowd alike enraged the rulers and hemmed them in: they longed to kill him but dared not, for the people’s astonishment shielded him. The witnesses see in this both the gathering storm of the Passion — the authorities now fixed on his death — and the restraining hand of providence, holding back their malice until his hour was come. Robertson notes both Sadducees and Pharisees combine here, the climax come right in the temple; the witnesses agree the cleansing brought the conflict to a head, Christ’s authority and his hold on the crowd alike enraging the rulers yet hemming them in, so that they longed to kill him but dared not, the people’s astonishment shielding him until his hour was come.
19:
‘And when even was come, he went out of the city.’ The witnesses note Christ’s nightly withdrawal and the fathers’ symbolic reading.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the construction means ‘whenever evening came on,’ the imperfect marking Christ’s custom of leaving the city each night (compare Luke 21:37). Ellicott marks the note of time as peculiar to Mark. Gill notes he went to Bethany or the Mount of Olives, taking the disciples.

The fathers read it symbolically. Pseudo-Jerome takes the Lord, ‘leaving darkness behind him in the hearts of the Jews,’ as the sun setting on one city to rise on another, the light taken from the scribes shining in the apostles. Theophylact and Bede read the withered fig tree, ‘dried up from the roots,’ as the synagogue smitten not for a time but for ever, stripped of the favour of heaven.

The Puritan Henry draws the lesson: ‘all wither who reject Christ’; the tree ‘represented the state of the Jewish church,’ and we should ‘rest in no religion that does not make us fruitful in good works.’ The shared, dominant note is that Christ withdrew from the hostile city at evening, as was his custom in that last week, and that the fig tree, found withered the next morning, becomes the text for his teaching on faith. The witnesses see in his nightly retreat both prudence amid mounting danger and the fathers’ symbol of the light departing from a fruitless people to shine elsewhere. Bede reads the fig tree dried from the roots as the synagogue smitten not for a time but for ever, stripped of the favour of heaven; the witnesses see in Christ’s nightly withdrawal both prudence amid mounting danger and the fathers’ symbol of the light departing from a fruitless people to shine in the apostles, the tree representing the state of the Jewish church. Henry draws the lesson that we should rest in no religion that does not make us fruitful in good works, the withered tree representing the state of the Jewish church. Pseudo-Jerome reads the Lord, leaving darkness in the hearts of the Jews, as the sun setting on one city to rise on another, the light shining in the apostles.
20:
‘And in the morning, as they passed by, they saw the fig tree dried up from the roots.’ The few brief voices note the completeness of the withering.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes they passed by ‘the next morning’ on the steep road down the Mount of Olives, and that ‘from the roots’ is Mark’s detail, with the perfect participle ‘dried up,’ where Matthew 21:20 does not separate the two mornings. Gill notes they saw it returning from Bethany or the Mount.

With only a few brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. On the Tuesday morning, passing the same way, the disciples saw the fig tree Christ had cursed now utterly withered, ‘dried up from the roots.’ Mark’s detail — the withering reaching to the very roots — marks the thoroughness of the judgment, and his separation of the two mornings gives the precise order of events.

The shared, dominant note is the swift and total fulfilment of Christ’s word. The tree that the day before had stood green and leafy was now dead to the root, a visible proof of the power of his curse and an emblem of the doom of fruitless profession. The witnesses see in the completeness of the withering — not a wilting of the leaves only but a death from the roots — both the certainty of Christ’s word and the picture, drawn out by the fathers, of a religion smitten not for a season but for ever, that the disciples, beholding, might learn the lesson of faith that follows. Robertson notes they passed by the next morning on the steep road down the Mount of Olives, ‘from the roots’ being Mark’s detail with the perfect participle; the witnesses see in the completeness of the withering — not a wilting of the leaves only but a death from the roots — both the certainty of Christ’s word and the picture of a religion smitten not for a season but for ever. Gill notes they saw it returning from Bethany or the Mount, and the witnesses agree Mark’s separation of the two mornings gives the precise order of events.
21:
‘Master, behold, the fig tree which thou cursedst is withered away.’ The few voices note Peter’s remembering exclamation.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘Peter calling to remembrance’ is ‘only in Mark and due to Peter’s story’ — his ‘quick memory’ — and that the verb ‘thou cursedst’ ‘almost sounds as if Peter blamed Jesus.’ Barnes explains ‘cursedst’ means ‘to devote to destruction,’ not implying blame. Gill notes Peter remembered chiefly ‘the imprecation of Christ upon it.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Peter, recognising the withered tree, exclaims with astonishment that the very tree Christ had cursed was dead. The grammatical commentators note the detail is one of Mark’s touches drawn from Peter’s own recollection, and that his words carry a note of wonder — perhaps even of surprise that the curse had taken such swift effect.

The shared, dominant note is that the withered tree provoked the disciples’ amazement and so opened the way for Christ’s teaching on faith. Peter’s exclamation — ‘the fig tree which thou cursedst is withered’ — both confirms the fulfilment of Christ’s word and reveals the disciples’ wonder at it. The witnesses see in the vividness of the report the stamp of the eyewitness, and in the disciples’ surprise the occasion for the Lord to turn their gaze from the marvel of the withered tree to the power of faith and prayer that could do far greater things. Barnes explains ‘cursedst’ means ‘to devote to destruction,’ not implying blame, and Gill that Peter remembered chiefly the imprecation of Christ; the witnesses see in the vividness of the report the stamp of the eyewitness, and in the disciples’ surprise the occasion for the Lord to turn their gaze from the marvel of the withered tree to the power of faith and prayer. Robertson notes the verb ‘thou cursedst’ almost sounds as if Peter blamed Jesus, the detail one of Mark’s touches drawn from Peter’s own recollection. Barnes explains the curse means the tree should be devoted to destruction, not implying any fault in the tree itself.
22:
‘And Jesus answering saith unto them, Have faith in God.’ The few voices note the lesson Christ draws from the withered tree.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the phrase. Barnes and Clarke note ‘have faith in God’ is literally ‘have the faith of God’ — a Hebraism for ‘strong faith’ or confidence that God can accomplish the most difficult things, as the tree withered by a word. Robertson reads the objective genitive (so Romans 3:22), the lesson for the disciples from the curse so promptly fulfilled. Gill notes the answer is to all the disciples, Peter having spoken for them.

The Wesleyan Wesley marvels that the Creator might destroy ‘a thousand of his inanimate creatures… were it only to imprint this important lesson more deeply on one immortal spirit.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ turns the disciples’ wonder at the withered tree into a call to faith. The miracle was not for its own sake but to teach them the power of believing prayer; ‘have faith in God’ — strong, confident faith — is the lesson of the sign.

The shared, dominant note is that the cursing of the fig tree was, above all, an object lesson in faith. The grammatical commentators stress the force of the Hebraism — not a weak or wavering trust but ‘the faith of God,’ the strongest confidence in his power. The witnesses see in Christ’s words the key to the whole episode: he would have his disciples learn from the withered tree that faith in God can do far greater things, as the verses following, on the removing of mountains and the answering of prayer, unfold. Robertson reads the objective genitive (so Romans 3:22), the lesson for the disciples from the curse so promptly fulfilled; the witnesses agree the cursing of the fig tree was, above all, an object lesson in faith — not a weak or wavering trust but ‘the faith of God,’ the strongest confidence in his power, that they might learn faith can do far greater things.
23:
‘Whosoever shall say unto this mountain, Be thou removed… and shall not doubt in his heart… he shall have whatsoever he saith.’ The witnesses note the great promise to undoubting faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes ‘shall not doubt in his heart’ means ‘a divided judgment’ — wavering doubt — set against ‘continued faith,’ and the futuristic present ‘cometh to pass.’ Ellicott notes the better text, ‘the thing which he saith cometh to pass,’ the promise specific before the wider word of v.24. Barnes refers to the parallel (Matthew 17:20), ‘this mountain’ being the Mount of Olives at hand.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is a catechetical passage on the ways of eternal life, and so does not directly bear on the verse.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ promises that faith without doubt can do what seems impossible — remove a mountain — and that the believer ‘shall have whatsoever he saith.’ The grammatical commentators stress the condition: not a single act of doubt but a settled, undivided faith.

The shared, dominant note is the immense power of believing prayer, hedged by the demand for undoubting faith. The promise is hyperbolical in form — the casting of a mountain into the sea — yet, the witnesses agree, true to the experience of faith, which sees the fulfilment before it happens. They note that such promises apply chiefly, as the next verse shows, to prayer offered in accordance with God’s will, and that the heart of the saying is the call to a faith that does not waver but rests wholly on the power and faithfulness of God. Barnes refers to the parallel (Matthew 17:20), ‘this mountain’ being the Mount of Olives at hand; the witnesses agree the promise, hyperbolical in form — the casting of a mountain into the sea — is yet true to the experience of faith, which sees the fulfilment before it happens, and applies chiefly to prayer offered according to God’s will.
24:
‘What things soever ye desire, when ye pray, believe that ye receive them, and ye shall have them.’ The witnesses note the great promise to believing prayer.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the faith required. Robertson notes the aorist ‘believe that ye received them’ is ‘the test of faith… that sees the fulfilment before it happens.’ Ellicott reads the better text, ‘believe that ye received them,’ and notes that ‘as a rule such words imply prayer for spiritual rather than temporal blessings,’ in which region ‘the subjective faith becomes an objective reality’ — for peace, pardon, illumination, the promise ‘is psychologically true.’ Barnes cites the parallels (Matthew 7:7, James 1:5-6).

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here treats the Holy Spirit and so does not directly bear on the verse. With so brief a body of genuine comment the synthesis is short, but the substance is clear and agreed: Christ promises that what is asked in believing prayer — with faith that already receives — shall be granted.

The shared, dominant note is the confidence proper to prayer. The grammatical commentators stress the striking tense — ‘believe that ye received’ — a faith that counts the gift already given; and Ellicott’s qualification, that such promises hold chiefly for spiritual blessings, where to believe is, in a sense, already to possess. The witnesses see in the verse the crown of Christ’s teaching from the fig tree: faith in God, expressed in believing prayer, lays hold of God’s gifts; and the believer is to ask not doubtfully but with the assurance that the Father hears and grants what accords with his will. Barnes cites the parallels (Matthew 7:7, James 1:5-6), and the witnesses agree the verse is the crown of Christ’s teaching from the fig tree: faith in God, expressed in believing prayer, lays hold of God’s gifts, the believer asking not doubtfully but with the assurance that the Father hears and grants what accords with his will. Ellicott notes such words imply chiefly spiritual rather than temporal blessings, in which region the subjective faith becomes an objective reality, the promise psychologically true.
25:
‘And when ye stand praying, forgive, if ye have ought against any: that your Father also… may forgive you.’ The witnesses note the link between forgiving and being forgiven, and the posture of prayer.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes the late form ‘whensoever ye stand,’ and that Jesus does not teach standing as the only posture, but that ‘God’s willingness to forgive is limited by our willingness to forgive others’ (recall Matthew 6:14). Barnes notes prayer was offered sometimes standing, sometimes kneeling, God looking on the heart; Clarke that the Pharisees ‘loved to pray standing.’

The Wesleyan Wesley sums the condition: forgiving, ‘ye shall have whatever you ask, without wrath or doubting.’ Gill notes standing was ‘an usual posture in praying.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: believing prayer must be joined to a forgiving heart, for the Father forgives those who forgive. The grammatical commentators note that the posture — standing or kneeling — is indifferent, God regarding the heart, but the duty of forgiveness is not.

The shared, dominant note is that an unforgiving spirit bars the way to forgiveness and answered prayer. Christ adds to the promise of believing prayer this solemn condition: the suppliant must himself forgive. The witnesses see in this both a guard against presuming on prayer while harbouring resentment, and a sobering thought, as Robertson says, for all who pray — that God’s pardon of us is bound up with our pardon of others, so that the prayer of faith and the forgiving heart must go together. Barnes notes prayer was offered sometimes standing, sometimes kneeling, God looking on the heart, and Clarke that the Pharisees loved to pray standing; the witnesses see in the verse both a guard against presuming on prayer while harbouring resentment, and a sobering thought that God’s pardon of us is bound up with our pardon of others, so that the prayer of faith and the forgiving heart go together. Robertson notes Jesus does not teach standing as the only posture, but that God’s willingness to forgive is limited by our willingness to forgive others (Matthew 6:14).
26:
‘But if ye do not forgive, neither will your Father… forgive your trespasses.’ The few voices note the stern converse and the textual question.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the text. Robertson observes the verse ‘is omitted by Westcott and Hort’ and put in a footnote by the Revisers; Clarke notes it is wanting in several manuscripts and versions. Barnes refers to the parallel (Matthew 18:35). Gill expounds the duty: to forgive ‘freely and fully… passing by the offences and injuries done you.’

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is a doctrinal passage on the angels and the vision of God, and so does not bear on the verse.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the converse of the promise stands — if we will not forgive, neither will the Father forgive us. The grammatical commentators note that the verse, though textually doubtful in Mark, teaches what is plainly taught elsewhere (Matthew 6:15).

The shared, dominant note is the solemn reciprocity of forgiveness. To refuse pardon to others is to forfeit the pardon of God; the unforgiving cannot themselves be forgiven. The witnesses see in this the negative side of the previous verse, sharpening its warning: prayer offered with an unforgiving heart not only fails of its answer but leaves the suppliant under his own unforgiven trespasses. Whatever the verse’s standing in the manuscripts, its doctrine is certain, that the measure we mete to others in forgiveness is the measure meted to us. Barnes refers to the parallel (Matthew 18:35), and Gill expounds the duty to forgive freely and fully; the witnesses see in the verse the negative side of the previous one, sharpening its warning, that prayer offered with an unforgiving heart not only fails of its answer but leaves the suppliant under his own unforgiven trespasses, whatever the verse’s standing in the manuscripts. Robertson observes the verse is omitted by Westcott and Hort, the Revisers putting it in a footnote, yet its doctrine is certain, taught plainly elsewhere (Matthew 6:15).
27:
‘By what authority doest thou these things? and who gave thee this authority?’ The witnesses note the formal challenge of the Sanhedrin to Christ’s authority.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Robertson notes the three groups — chief priests, scribes, and elders — were ‘a large committee of the Sanhedrin… in a formal attack’ on his authority for cleansing and teaching in the temple. Henry notes they claimed power ‘to call prophets to an account,’ and came as if to arraign him at the bar.

The fathers probe the malice. Theophylact notes they came ‘to question and tempt him,’ hoping to catch him whichever way he answered. Bede observes that in asking ‘by what authority’ they ‘doubt its being the power of God… and wish it understood’ to be the devil’s work, denying he is the Son of God; but the Lord ‘prudently puts them a question, that they might be condemned either by their silence or their speaking.’

The Wesleyan Clarke and Gill note Christ was ‘not in the least intimidated’ by the assembled rulers. The shared, dominant note is that the Sanhedrin, stung by the cleansing, formally demanded Christ’s credentials, hoping to trap him: if he claimed divine authority they would charge blasphemy, if human they would discredit him. The witnesses see in the challenge both the rulers’ refusal to read the plain evidence of his works and the wisdom of Christ, who meets their snare not with a direct claim but with a counter-question that exposes their own bad faith. Theophylact notes they came to question and tempt him, and Henry that they claimed power to call prophets to account; the witnesses see in the challenge both the rulers’ refusal to read the plain evidence of his works and the wisdom of Christ, who meets their snare not with a direct claim but with a counter-question that exposes their own bad faith. Bede observes that in asking ‘by what authority’ they doubt its being the power of God, wishing it thought the devil’s work, but the Lord prudently puts them a question that condemns them by their silence or their speaking.
28:
‘By what authority doest thou these things?’ The few brief voices note the legitimacy and the intent of the question.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the question ‘in all three Gospels was a perfectly legitimate one’ (so Matthew 21:23-27), the present subjunctive meaning ‘that you keep on doing these things.’ Gill explains ‘these things’ as entering the temple as its Lord, correcting abuses, working miracles, and teaching the people. Barnes refers to the like challenge to Moses (Numbers 16:3).

With only three brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. The rulers press their demand: by what authority, and from whom, does Jesus act as he does — cleansing the temple, teaching, working wonders? The question, the grammatical commentators note, was in itself fair, for the Sanhedrin had a recognised duty to examine those who claimed a prophetic or teaching office.

The shared, dominant note is that a legitimate question was asked in an illegitimate spirit. The rulers were entitled to inquire into Christ’s authority, but they came not to learn but to trap, having already resolved to destroy him. The witnesses see in ‘these things’ the whole sweep of his recent acts — the royal entry, the cleansing, the teaching — each a claim to an authority they would not acknowledge; and in their demand the refusal of men who, having abundant evidence of his works, still would not believe, and so sought a pretext against him rather than the truth. Gill explains ‘these things’ as entering the temple as its Lord, correcting abuses, working miracles, and teaching; the witnesses see in their demand the refusal of men who, having abundant evidence of his works, still would not believe, and so sought a pretext against him rather than the truth, a legitimate question asked in an illegitimate spirit. Barnes refers to the like challenge to Moses (Numbers 16:3), and the witnesses agree the question, fair in itself, was asked by men resolved not to learn but to trap him. Robertson notes the present subjunctive means ‘that you keep on doing these things,’ the rulers pressing their demand for his credentials.
29:
‘I will also ask of you one question, and answer me.’ Only two brief voices comment, so the synthesis is short; they note Christ’s fearless counter-question.

The Reformed Gill notes Christ answered ‘being not in the least intimidated’ by the assembled rulers, and the grammatical-historical Barnes that he met their demand with ‘one question’ (or ‘one thing’) of his own.

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Christ, far from evading or fearing the Sanhedrin’s challenge, answers it with a counter-question, on the answer to which he will make his own reply depend. The move is no dodge but a wise turning of the tables, putting the rulers to the test before they test him.

The shared, dominant note is the calm mastery of Christ before his accusers. Undaunted by the formal deputation, he proposes a single question of his own, fair and to the point, that will expose the bad faith of his questioners. The witnesses see in this both his fearlessness — he stands unintimidated before the rulers of the nation — and his wisdom, for the question he is about to ask, concerning the baptism of John, will catch them in the very snare they had laid for him, so that their refusal to answer honestly absolves him from answering them, while it lays bare their own unbelief. The witnesses agree Christ, far from evading or fearing the Sanhedrin’s challenge, answers it with a counter-question, on the answer to which he will make his own reply depend; the move is no dodge but a wise turning of the tables, putting the rulers to the test before they test him, and exposing the bad faith of his questioners. Gill notes Christ answered being not in the least intimidated by the assembled rulers, and Barnes that he met their demand with one question of his own, putting them to the test. The witnesses agree the move was no dodge but a wise turning of the tables, exposing the bad faith of his questioners before they could test him. The question concerning John’s baptism, which he is about to put, will catch them in the very snare they had laid for him.
30:
‘The baptism of John, was it from heaven, or of men? answer me.’ The two voices note the searching dilemma Christ poses.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the sharp demand ‘answer me’ is ‘only in Mark,’ and that Jesus ‘has a right to take this turn because of John’s direct relation to himself’: it was ‘not a dodge, but a home thrust… They rejected John as they now reject Jesus.’ Gill notes John was the first preacher and administrator of his baptism.

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Christ poses the rulers a dilemma: was John’s baptism from heaven or of men? The question is no evasion but goes to the heart of the matter, for John had borne witness to Jesus, so that to judge John rightly was to judge Jesus rightly.

The shared, dominant note is the aptness and force of Christ’s counter-question. By binding the question of his own authority to that of John’s — the forerunner who had pointed to him — he makes their answer about the one decide the other. The witnesses see in ‘answer me’ the directness of the home thrust: the rulers, who had rejected John, are now forced either to own a heavenly authority they had spurned or to deny it before the people who revered him. Their treatment of the forerunner had already settled their treatment of the Christ. Gill notes John was the first preacher and administrator of his baptism; the witnesses agree the question is no evasion but goes to the heart of the matter, for John had borne witness to Jesus, so that to judge John rightly was to judge Jesus rightly, and the rulers’ treatment of the forerunner had already settled their treatment of the Christ. Robertson notes the sharp ‘answer me’ is only in Mark, a home thrust binding John’s authority to Christ’s, since they rejected John as they now reject Jesus. Gill notes the doctrine and ordinance of baptism began with John, so that to weigh his commission was to weigh the witness he bore to Jesus.
31:
‘And they reasoned with themselves… If we shall say, From heaven; he will say, Why then did ye not believe him?’ The two voices note the rulers’ secret dilemma.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes they ‘see the two horns of the dilemma clearly and poignantly… They know only too well what Jesus will say.’ Gill notes they withdrew a little to consult privately. They wished ‘to break Christ’s power with the multitude, but a false step now will turn the laugh on them.’

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. The rulers, caught, reason among themselves: to say John’s baptism was from heaven would convict them of unbelief, for they had not received him. The first horn of the dilemma is fatal to them, and they know it.

The shared, dominant note is the self-condemnation of the rulers in their secret counsel. Their reasoning betrays that they judged not by truth but by expediency, calculating only how to escape the trap without loss of face or popularity. The witnesses see in this the exposure of their bad faith: they had asked Christ by what authority he acted, but could not honestly answer the like question about John, whose heavenly commission they had refused. Their unwillingness to confess the truth they knew — that John was from heaven — lays bare the unbelief that lay behind their challenge. Gill notes they withdrew a little to consult privately, wishing to break Christ’s power with the multitude; the witnesses see in this the exposure of their bad faith, that they judged not by truth but by expediency, calculating only how to escape the trap, their unwillingness to confess the truth they knew laying bare the unbelief that lay behind their challenge. Robertson notes they see the two horns of the dilemma clearly and poignantly, knowing only too well what Jesus will say, and so judge by expediency rather than truth. Gill notes they wished to break Christ’s power with the multitude, but saw that a false step now would turn the laugh upon themselves.
32:
‘But if we shall say, Of men; they feared the people: for all men counted John, that he was a prophet indeed.’ The witnesses note the second horn of the dilemma and the people’s reverence for John.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes the rugged construction with ‘anacoluthon or even aposiopesis’ — ‘they feared the people’ — and that all three Gospels state the popular view of John as a prophet; his martyrdom had ‘deepened his power over the people.’ Clarke notes the reading ‘we fear the people,’ and Ellicott that Mark’s intensifying ‘a prophet indeed’ is one of his ‘graphic touches.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the other horn was as deadly — to call John’s baptism merely human would enrage the people, who held him a true prophet (Luke adds, they would stone them). Caught between conviction and popularity, the rulers dared not speak.

The shared, dominant note is the rulers’ entrapment between truth and fear. They could not own John’s heavenly authority without condemning their own unbelief, nor deny it without facing the wrath of the people who revered him even in death. The witnesses see in this the just recompense of their bad faith: the men who came to trap Christ are themselves trapped, unable to answer a plain question about the forerunner without exposing either their unbelief or their cowardice, and so reduced to a feigned ignorance. Clarke notes the reading ‘we fear the people,’ and Ellicott that Mark’s intensifying ‘a prophet indeed’ is one of his graphic touches, Luke adding that the people would stone them; the witnesses see in this the just recompense of their bad faith, the men who came to trap Christ themselves trapped, unable to answer a plain question without exposing either their unbelief or their cowardice. Robertson notes the rugged construction with aposiopesis, ‘they feared the people,’ John’s martyrdom having deepened his power over the people even in death. Clarke notes the reading ‘we fear the people,’ the rulers caught between conviction and the wrath of those who revered John as a prophet indeed.
33:
‘We cannot tell. And Jesus… saith… Neither do I tell you by what authority I do these things.’ The witnesses note the rulers’ feigned ignorance and Christ’s just refusal.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes their ‘We know not’ was ‘to get out of the trap,’ their ‘self-imposed ignorance… absolved Jesus from a categorical reply,’ though ‘he has no notion of letting them off.’ Ellicott notes the real stress lies on the pronoun, ‘Neither do I tell you,’ and that the better rendering is ‘we do not know.’ Barnes ties their willful ignorance to the judicial blindness of Isaiah 29:14.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the rulers, refusing to answer honestly, plead ignorance — ‘we cannot tell’ — and Christ, with perfect justice, declines to tell them by what authority he acts. Their evasion forfeits their right to an answer.

The shared, dominant note is the righteous turning of the rulers’ snare back upon themselves. By their cowardly ‘we cannot tell’ they confess themselves unfit judges of John, and so of Jesus; their self-chosen ignorance absolves him from satisfying their malice. The witnesses see in Christ’s reply no evasion but justice: men who will not own the truth they know about the forerunner have no claim to be taught the authority of the Christ. Their feigned ignorance, the grammatical commentators note, was itself a judgment — the wise men of Israel, refusing the light they had, were left in the darkness they chose. Ellicott notes the real stress lies on the pronoun, ‘Neither do I tell you,’ and Barnes ties their willful ignorance to judicial blindness (Isaiah 29:14); the witnesses see in Christ’s reply no evasion but justice, men who will not own the truth they know about the forerunner having no claim to be taught the authority of the Christ, their feigned ignorance itself a judgment. Robertson notes their self-imposed ignorance absolved Jesus from a categorical reply, though he has no notion of letting them off, the wise men of Israel left in the darkness they chose.
